questions.prompt: Complete: "This is ___ book in the whole library."
questions.explanation: "In the whole library" marks the top of a group, so use the superlative with the: the oldest book. "older" is the comparative (for two things), "more old" misuses more on a short adjective, and a superlative needs the, so "oldest" alone is incomplete.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Of all my friends, Sami is ___."
questions.explanation: Funny ends in consonant + y, so the superlative is the funniest (y → i, then -est). "the funnyest" keeps the y wrongly, "funnier" is the comparative, and "the most funny" treats a short -y adjective as long, which it isn't.

questions.prompt: Complete: "A plane is ___ way to travel between countries."
questions.explanation: There is no than here and we mean the top way of all, so use the superlative: the fastest way. "faster" needs a than (two things), "more fast" misuses more on a short adjective, and a superlative needs the, so "fastest" alone is wrong.

Important is a long adjective, so the comparative is more important than. "importanter" wrongly adds -er to a long adjective, and "the most important"/"most important" are superlatives, which don't go with than.

questions.prompt: Complete: "That was ___ meal I have ever eaten."
questions.explanation: Good is irregular: its superlative is the best (the best meal I have ever eaten). "the goodest" and "the most good" invent a regular form that doesn't exist, and "the better" is the comparative form, used for two things, not the top of a group.

options.text: She is the most tallest in her class.
She is the tallest in her class.
She is tallest in her class.
She is more tallest in her class.

questions.explanation: Tall is short, so the superlative is the tallest in her class. "the most tallest" double-marks it (most + -est), "more tallest" mixes comparative and superlative, and "tallest" without the is incomplete — a superlative needs the.
